items.title: A look at every player who had a positive EPM in the 2026 NBA playoffs categorized by position.
items.body_excerpt:
A look at every player who had a positive EPM in the 2026 NBA playoffs categorized by position. 

Data: Dunksandthrees.com 

Glossary; Estimated Plus-Minus (EPM) is an all-in-one player metric that predicts a player's contribution to the team in points per 100 possessions.

images.alt:
Alt text:

"Bar chart titled 'Estimated Plus-Minus by Position,' subtitled '2026 NBA Playoffs, players with a positive playoff EPM, darker equals higher EPM.' Five columns for PG, SG, SF, PF, and C list players ranked highest to lowest EPM, with bar shading darkening as values rise.

PG: Jalen Brunson +3.2, Shai Gilgeous-Alexander +3.1, Dylan Harper +2.9, Donte DiVincenzo +2.4, Cade Cunningham +1.7, Jose Alvarado +1.7, Mike Conley +1.2, James Harden +1.1, Marcus Smart +0.8, Payton Pritchard +0.6, De'Aaron Fox +0.4, Tyrese Maxey +0.0.

SG: Ajay Mitchell +4.8, Cason Wallace +3.1, Stephon Castle +1.1, RJ Barrett +0.9, VJ Edgecombe +0.5, Donovan Mitchell +0.2.

SF: Alex Caruso +3.8, Mikal Bridges +2.6, Ausar Thompson +2.5, Devin Vassell +2.3, Josh Hart +1.9, Tari Eason +1.1, Franz Wagner +1.0, Paul George +0.5, LeBron James +0.5.

PF: OG Anunoby +3.7, Scottie Barnes +3.2, Jayson Tatum +3.1, Julian Champagnie +1.2, Paolo Banchero +1.1, Dillon Brooks +0.9, Jalen Williams +0.3, Rui Hachimura +0.3, Evan Mobley +0.2, Dean Wade +0.1.

C: Victor Wembanyama +9.4, Karl-Anthony Towns +8.0, Paul Reed +3.2, Isaiah Hartenstein +2.4, Jarrett Allen +2.3, Chet Holmgren +2.0, Robert Williams III +1.7, Nikola Jokic +1.2, Wendell Carter Jr. +0.9, Luka Garza +0.3.

Footer: Data from DunksAndThrees.com, visualization by HoopInformatics.bsky.social."

items.title: Hollander POTD Alan Ogg. Big man from Alabama-Birmingham was undrafted signing by MIA in 1990. Played there thru 1993. Could block shots well but was thin & didn't have other skills to really stick. Played in the CBA/abroad thru 1997. Passed away too young in 2009. RIP.

items.title: The 2004 Detroit Pistons championship often gets cited as an underdog championship, but they were the second best team in basketball (point differential) that season while the runner-up Lakers were 7th.
items.body_excerpt:
The 2004 Detroit Pistons championship often gets cited as an underdog championship, but they were the second best team in basketball (point differential) that season while the runner-up Lakers were 7th. 

Screenshot: CleaningTheGlass.com

images.alt: Table from Cleaning the Glass ranking the top 10 NBA teams by point differential for the 2003-04 regular season. League average is 41.0 wins and 41.0 losses. San Antonio ranks 1st at +8.0 (57-25). Detroit 2nd at +7.7 (54-28). Indiana 3rd at +7.1 (61-21). Minnesota 4th at +6.9 (58-24). Sacramento 5th at +6.2 (55-27). Dallas 6th at +4.4 (52-30). LA Lakers 7th at +4.0 (56-26). New Jersey 8th at +3.5 (47-35). Memphis 9th at +3.1 (50-32). Houston 10th at +2.0 (45-37).

items.title: One problem in the streaming era is that viewers always seem to be taken by surprise by games/events airing exclusively on streaming services.
items.body_excerpt:
One problem in the streaming era is that viewers always seem to be taken by surprise by games/events airing exclusively on streaming services.

Not sure that's fixable -- people are still surprised by CFB/CBB title games being on cable, and that's been going on for a decade-plus.

items.title: Bonus Hollander POTD Adrian Caldwell. Lamar F was undrafted in 89 but made HOU in camp. Lasted thru 91 as deep bench guy. Played in Europe from 91-95 & returned to NBA from 95-97 (w/NY, IND, PHI & DAL). Best NBA season was 96-97 (13 mpg, 2 ppg, 4 rpg). Good boarder & defender.

items.title: (no text)

images.alt:
With a little over two minutes in Game 5 of the Finals and the Knicks up one, I burst into tears. I wasn't sad, and they weren't tears of joy; the outcome was nowhere near settled.
What was this feeling? This was something new. Finding no clarity within, I looked for a parallel without.
The Knicks were winning the game. They were winning the championship. That wasn't a snowball in hell or a dark horse or a betting favorite. It wasn't a thought, it was, new and boundless and ineffable, like their play all postseason. There was no time for distance, no space to contain the emotion, no context to compare it against.

Something I was thinking about when Team USA kept trying to switch w/ Jay Huff: 

The NBA averaged the most possessions w/ at least 3 offensive players crashing last season in the tracking era. Are switching bigs as valuable the more teams value possession maxxing?

items.title: Joan Beringer: "I never played the four before in my life, so it's a big change. But I feel good about [playing the fou] and playing with Rocco, so I'm very excited."

items.title: Hollinger POTD Pau Gasol. Spanish big was drafted by 3rd by MEM in 01. Was immediate starter & won ROY. Became regular all-star & was best player on 1st good MEM squads. Traded to LAL in 08, helped Kobe to 2 titles. Also great in int'l play. Now a HOFer.

Fever held the Aces to 68 points, a season-low allowed. Just 27 of them came in the second half. Fever's week off ended up giving them a legs advantage, and they rode it to a win. They're 12-8 now.

Kelsey Mitchell with 27. Aliyah Boston with 18 and 10 boards.
